coredata.dc:title: Detection and remediation of mercury contaminated environment by nanotechnology: Progress and challenges
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yonghua
Chen, Hanqing
Zhu, Nali
Zhang, Jing
Li, Yufeng
Xu, Diandou
Gao, Yuxi
Zhao, Jiating
coredata.dc:description: Hg pollution is a global concern due to its high ecotoxicity and health risk to human beings. A comprehensive understanding of the fast-developed technology applied in determining and controlling Hg pollution is beneficial for risk assessment and field remediation. Herein, we mainly assembled the recent progress on Hg treatment in the environment by nanotechnology. The advantages and disadvantages of the conventional and nanotechnology-based methods commonly used in water-/soil-Hg remediation were compared and summarized. Specifically, green nanomaterials derived from plant tissues (e.g., nanocellulose) have prominent merits in remediation of Hg contaminated environments, including high efficiency in Hg removal, low cost, environment-friendly, and easily degradable. Based on the theories of Hg biogeochemistry and existed researches, four promising pathways are proposed, 1) developing surface-modified green nanocellulose with high selectivity and affinity towards Hg; 2) designing effective dispersants in preventing nanocellulose from agglomeration in soil; 3) mediating soil properties by adding green nanomaterials-based fertilizers; 4) improving plant-Hg-extract capacity with green nanomaterials addition. Briefly, more efficient and available approaches are still expected to be developed and implemented in the natural environment for Hg remediation.

head.ce:title: Detection and remediation of mercury contaminated environment by nanotechnology: Progress and challenges

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hg pollution is a global concern due to its high ecotoxicity and health risk to human beings. A comprehensive understanding of the fast-developed technology applied in determining and controlling Hg pollution is beneficial for risk assessment and field remediation. Herein, we mainly assembled the recent progress on Hg treatment in the environment by nanotechnology. The advantages and disadvantages of the conventional and nanotechnology-based methods commonly used in water-/soil-Hg remediation were compared and summarized. Specifically, green nanomaterials derived from plant tissues (e.g., nanocellulose) have prominent merits in remediation of Hg contaminated environments, including high efficiency in Hg removal, low cost, environment-friendly, and easily degradable. Based on the theories of Hg biogeochemistry and existed researches, four promising pathways are proposed, 1) developing surface-modified green nanocellulose with high selectivity and affinity towards Hg; 2) designing effective dispersants in preventing nanocellulose from agglomeration in soil; 3) mediating soil properties by adding green nanomaterials-based fertilizers; 4) improving plant-Hg-extract capacity with green nanomaterials addition. Briefly, more efficient and available approaches are still expected to be developed and implemented in the natural environment for Hg remediation.

ce:section.ce:para: Mercury (Hg) is recognized as a global pollutant due to its widespread occurrences, refractory properties, high bioaccumulation, and toxicity to ecosystems and human health (Grilo et al., 2015). Weathering of geologic reservoirs into the biosphere and anthropogenic release are two key pathways for Hg contamination in aqueous environments and croplands (Amos et al., 2013). Power plants were estimated to emit 16% of the anthropogenic Hg emission to air in 2010, which is the main source of global mercury emission (Liu et al., 2018)). As the largest coal consumer, Hg emissions from coal-fired power plants in China have increased public concern (Jiang et al., 2006). Many studies have already been aimed to reveal the transformation, transportation, and deposition of different Hg species, as well as their association with the safety of the biosphere (Driscoll et al., 2013). The toxicity of Hg is highly dependent on its species, including inorganic Hg (IHg, e.g., Hg2+ and Hg+) and methylmercury (MeHg, with high neurotoxicity) (Qiu et al., 2012). Seafood, such as tuna, swordfish, grouper, and mackerel, is considered the main entry point for MeHg into the human body, which impacts approximately one billion people worldwide (Nicklisch et al., 2017). Recent studies reported that consumption of rice and vegetables is another important pathway for human Hg, especially MeHg, exposure in South China, and raises health risk to local residents (Li et al., 2012; Zhang et al., 2010). Discharge of industrial wastewater and atmospheric deposition of airborne Hg was evidenced as the main pathways for Hg contamination in croplands (Driscoll et al., 2007). After entering water and soil, Hg (0) and Hg ions can be oxidized and transformed to mercury sulfide (HgS) or methylated in the presence of dissolved organic matter (DOM) and hgcA/B-containing microbes, and MeHg can be degraded by merA/B-containing microbes and solar irradiation, through which way the net MeHg production can be modulated (Barkay et al., 2003; Li et al., 2020a,b). Accumulation of total Hg (HgT) and MeHg in crops thereby can be mitigated through immobilizing Hg (e.g., formation of HgS) and demethylation processes.

Removal of Hg in the environment to protect populations from the potential risk of its exposure has received global interest. To date, many methods and remediation strategies have been developed to remediate Hg-containing soil or sediments, such as incineration, precipitation, coagulation, distillation, ultrafiltration, roasting, solvent extraction, photocatalysis, and adsorption (Wang et al., 2012, 2019). However, many of these techniques for Hg remediation need high energy demand during the process operation. Besides, due to the high implementation costs, some strategies cannot be widely applied worldwide. The thermal desorption technology and plant extraction studied later are effective and environmentally friendly, but expensive or time-consuming when used for Hg polluted field remediation, which cannot meet the current state and need of the local economy and treatment policies (Wang et al., 2012). Therefore, it is urgent to develop novel methods that are of high efficiency, low cost, eco-friendly and can be applied worldwide to prevent Hg pollution in the environment, thereby reducing the potential risk of Hg to human health through food consumption.

Nanoparticles have received considerable attention in biomedical applications, such as biosensors, diagnosis, drug delivery, photoacoustic imaging, tumor theranostics, and therapy because of their fascinating physicochemical properties (Bhattacharya and Mukherjee, 2008; Van Rie and Thielemans, 2017; Suresh et al., 2010; Samanta and Medintz, 2016). Many environmental applications, such as the remediation of metal-contaminated soils and waters using nanoparticles, have been attempted recently due to their large surface area, high adsorption capacity, fast diffusion rate, tunable surface functionalities, and significant chemical reactivity (Araújo et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2020a,b). With the development of nanotechnology, nanomaterial-based strategies have also been realized in the prevention of Hg pollution in the environment. For instance, graphene oxide, carbon nanotubes, carboxymethyl cellulose, and chitosan have been used as the adsorbent for the removal of Hg from polluted water (Lu and Astruc, 2018). Many types of magnetic iron oxide nanoparticles including magnetite and maghemite nanoparticles are reported as small scavengers for Hg removal via chemical precipitation (Sadegh et al., 2018). Moreover, organic nanoparticles have received widespread attention for use in sensing Hg(II), and show potential ability in the adsorption of Hg(II) iron (Shirzadmehr et al., 2015). As one of the fast developed technology in recent decades, in this review, a comprehensive understanding of Hg remediation by nanotechnology is expected. The recent progress of nanotechnology-based strategies in the prevention of Hg pollution in the environment is summarized with the aim to shed light on the future studies in developing more effective and environmentally friendly materials for Hg remediation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nanotechnology based methods in Hg determination
ce:section.ce:para: Hg pollution in the environment and living organisms have attracted considerable attention throughout the world for decades, and the removal of Hg is the leading concern for human health by the public. Many studies have reported that Hg exhibited high toxicity to aquatic organisms and human health even at very low concentrations (Kumar, 2017). Accurate detection is the first step for Hg risk assessment in the environment. However, Hg, as a refractory heavy metal pollutant can be present in the atmosphere, water, and soil in various species, such as Hg0, Hg(II), S-/C-/O–Hg-R, and MeHg, and can be transformed between these species (Balshaw et al., 2007). These factors make it a great challenge for Hg determination and risk assessment in the natural environment. To date, many analytical methods have been developed for Hg detection in the environment, such as inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry (ICP-OES), cold vapor technique with atomic absorption spectrometry (CV-AAS), and cold vapor technique with atomic fluorescence spectrometry (CV-AFS), (Chandrasoma et al., 2012; Ding et al., 2013). However, these methods have apparent drawbacks, restrictions, or limitations in the determination of Hg, including large sample volume needs, high costs, time-consuming in sample pretreatment processes, long duration of testing, labor-intensive, and require bulky instrumentation, which greatly limits their practical application for the rapid on-site and real-time analyses (de Wuilloud et al., 2002). Therefore, how to improve the detection sensitivity and develop new analytical approaches of low Hg in the natural environment is an urgent task.

Recently, many Hg ion sensors were developed based on organic molecules, proteins, oligonucleotides, and engineered bacteria (Xie et al., 2012). However, many of these materials cannot meet the wide need due to their low water solubility, poor selectivity, and sensitivity. Due to the rapid development of nanotechnology, strategies based on nanomaterials are developed and applied for the detection of Hg in the environment. Some of the metal nanoparticles have been well designed for probing Hg ions in sewage and sediment thanks to their catalytic activity to form a solid amalgam-like structure (Guo et al., 2011). Nowadays, the development of nanomaterials as chemosensors, biosensors, and/or adsorbents for the simultaneous multiple signal amplification detection and fast removal of Hg has been reported and summarized in Table 1 . It is noted that the larger surface area, high sensitivity and selectivity, and rapid analysis, are the typical properties of these nanomaterials for Hg determination. Modification of these nanomaterials with various inorganic (e.g., metal ions) and organic groups (e.g., carbon and sulfur-containing molecules) endows their specific functions in Hg species selection, increased sensitivity, and high affinity for different forms of Hg in sewage and sediment (Kango et al., 2013).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Removal of Hg from contaminated water by nanomaterials
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the rapid development of industry and the continuous growth of population, the widespread diffusion of surface water and groundwater pollutants has become a key global issue (Xu et al., 2012). Generally, aquatic ecosystems are the hot spots for Hg transportation, transformation, and accumulation in food chains (de Almeida Rodrigues et al., 2019). The World Health Organization (WHO) suggests the maximum acceptable concentration of HgT in drinking water to be lower than 1 μg/L (Azimi and Moghaddam, 2013). The United States Environmental Protection Agency (USEPA) has been reported that a maximum limit of HgT in specific populations is 2.3 μg/g (Song et al., 2018). Thus, the removal of Hg from contaminated water has received high attention. Many effective methods, including chemical precipitation, coagulation, ultrafiltration, ion exchange, electrochemical recovery, bioremediation, solvent extraction, photocatalysis, and adsorption by activated carbon, have been developed in Hg removal from wastewater to reach the maximum limits established by the legislation before discharge (Nanseu-Njiki et al., 2009). However, most of the conventional techniques are time-consuming and require high temperature or tedious procedures, which limit their use in Hg remediation in low Hg contaminated sites.

Nanotechnology has been developed to reduce some metal pollutants to “near-zero” levels (e.g., Cr, Cu, and Hg) (Yadav et al., 2021), which provides fast, cheap, and environment-friendly strategies to eliminate Hg pollution in water samples. For instance, magnetic nanoparticles coated with nanometric silver and functionalized with sodium 1-mercaptoethane sulfonate were used to adsorb Hg(II) from aqueous samples and exhibited a 100% efficiency of Hg removal in 30 s (Vicente-Martínez et al., 2020). Wang et al. found that bacterial extracellular polymeric substances (EPS) can be used as an effective natural dispersant for Se nanoparticles to enhance the efficiency of Hg immobilization in contaminated waters by forming the stable HgSe (Wang et al., 2018). The removal of Hg ions from the Persian Gulf water by a hyperbranched and multi-functionalized dendrimer modified mixed-oxide nanoparticles was feasible, spontaneous, and chemically exothermic in nature between 15 and 80 °C (Arshadi et al., 2017). Coal fly ash-derived zeolites doped with silver nanoparticles were successfully applied for Hg(II) removal from the water, and exhibited high (<99%) and fast adsorption ability (Tauanov et al., 2018). The recent progress in nanomaterials for the application in Hg removal in Hg-containing water is assembled in Table 2 . The mechanisms for Hg removal by these nanomaterials mainly include the enhanced adsorption capacity, chemical precipitation, cation exchange, and high photocatalytic and electrolysis activities which would result in Hg transformation in aquatic environments. The advantages of these nanomaterials used in water-Hg removal are also prominent, such as high Hg removal capability, high chemical activity to react with Hg, high selectivity for Hg binding. However, the high cost and complicated follow-up treatment of the nanomaterial-Hg complexes should be the shortages, which will limit the wide use of artificial nanomaterials in the Hg removal.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nanomaterials on the treatment of Hg pollution in farmlands
ce:section.ce:para: Besides, the application of nanotechnology can also play an important role in improving agriculture and food production and show great advantages in maintaining nutrition and reducing Hg accumulation in crops (Duhan et al., 2017). For example, Nanoactivated carbon (NAC) as a soil amendment was reported to decrease Hg uptake by rice plants, reducing the Hg content of polished rice by 47–63% compared to the control to a lower level than the food safety value (20 ng g−1) defined by the Chinese government (Wang et al., 2020). Konate et al. (2017) found that the application of Fe3O4 NPs enhanced wheat growth and decreased heavy metal uptake by roots. However, the use of nanomaterials to repair Hg contaminated soil and sediment has received little attention and is still highly challenging, because there is a strong tendency for nanoparticle agglomeration in the soil or sediment solution which results in limited efficacy in the remediation of Hg-contaminated soils (Park et al., 2018). Additionally, nanoparticles are easily fixed on various solid substrates in the soil (Wang et al., 2018). To maintain the dispersion of nanoparticles, therefore, is very critical before they can be widely and effectively employed. In previous studies, well-dispersed silica nanoparticles and elemental selenium nanoparticles were reported to greatly alleviate Hg phytotoxicity by immobilizing Hg and inhibiting the transformation of Hg(II) to MeHg in soybean (Glycine max) and garlic (Allium sativum L.), along with a significant decrease in HgT and MeHg accumulation in plants (Li et al., 2020; Zhao et al., 2020). Moreover, fixation of Hg by nanomaterials on leaves has also been studied to prevent its translocation and reduce the Hg-induced phytotoxicity (Li et al., 2020). In previous work, selenium nanomaterials were sprayed onto the plant leaves where selenium combines with the airborne Hg and hinders their uptake by leaves. A portion of selenium nanoparticles can be absorbed by leaves, transported down to the roots, and then complex with soil-Hg in the rhizosphere (Wang et al., 2019a,b) (Fig. 2 ). Through mediating the biogenesis of HgS nanoparticles in plant leaves from atmospheric gaseous Hg is another pathway to prevent the uptake, translocation, and accumulation of Hg in plants (Manceau et al., 2018).

Hg contamination which is widespread and difficult to be efficiently managed has attracted increasing concerns in the world. Some conventional physical and chemical methods, e.g., thermal degradation and sulfide addition, were reported to be useful and acceptable in treating Hg contaminated water and soil to some extent, but cannot be applied in extensive field remediation because these methods induced physical and chemical changes of the soil. In recent years, fast-developed nanotechnology provides a new choice and opportunity to be employed in heavy metal pollution remediation. Thereinto, many nanomaterials have prominent advantages and are of high potential in Hg remediation of contaminated water and soil. To date, a lot of artificial nanomaterials have been designed with high efficiency in Hg determination and removal, especially applied in Hg polluted water systems. However, nanomaterials used in Hg-contaminated soils are still quite limited due to the high complexity of farmland conditions and the variety and refractory properties of Hg. Soil is a complex and heterogeneous system with multiple biotic and abiotic substances. Nanomaterials are prone to accumulate and agglomerate in soil particles and soil pore-water. Although some dispersants have been designed, they have not been widely used because of the high cost and low selectivity for Hg removal.

Given the above-mentioned challenges and based on the present literature on Hg biogeochemical behaviors and development of nanotechnology, here, we propose four pathways that might be of great potential in the remediation of Hg contaminated soils. 1) Developing nanocellulose materials with high efficiency, low cost, easily degradable, and environmentally friendly properties. The nanocellulose materials can be commonly prepared from plant tissues, such as straws and rice husks, and maybe surface modified by some active groups before use to improve their adsorption and complexing capacities for Hg removal and detoxification. 2) Designing effective and stable dispersants and surface modification reagents to prevent the nanocellulose materials from agglomeration and improve the selectivity towards Hg in field conditions. 3) Improving the soil-Hg resistance ability by mediating soil microbes activity and soil properties. Suitable addition of nanocellulose-like species can affect the soil properties, e.g., modification of the DOM composition and mediation of the community and activity of soil microorganisms, which in turn indirectly alleviating soil Hg contamination. 4) Developing phytoremediation technology accompanied by advanced farming measures. Phytoremediation is considered a high environment-friendly approach for soil-heavy metal treatment. However, there is still no available plant species evidenced to be efficient in Hg extract in contaminated soils. Therefore, besides continuing to seek for Hg hyperaccumulator, it should be more easily realized to enhance the ability of Hg extraction for currently known plants with high Hg resistance ability through molecular biological techniques and chemical means. Moreover, the application of some chemical reagents may help in mobilizing refractory Hg species to promote the plant-Hg-extract. In summary, combined employment of these conventional and promising approaches would play great roles in Hg remediation and removal in contaminated environments. The reaction mechanisms and practicability of the emerging advanced nanotechnology are still needed to be further unraveled and implemented in field studies.

All the contributors of this work have approved the final version of this manuscript, and declare no financial and personal relationships with other people or organizations that can influence this work. All the authors of this work also declare that there is no personal interest with any other communities or institutes if this manuscript could be accepted.

Yonghua Liu and Hanqing Chen, data processing and paper writing; Nali Zhu, Jing Zhang, Yufeng Li, Diandou Xu, and Yuxi Gao, paper writing, data collecting, review and revision; Jiating Zhao, paper writing, supervision, review and editing.
